Structure Editor Processing

Structure Editor Processing

Structure editor processing is dependent on the context, meaning that structure editor functions are not processed until certain conditions are met. The structure editor recognizes the context and if it is not correct, activates appropriate triggers to set the context correctly, for example, by validating data. If errors are encountered, further processing may be stopped.

Every user input event is actually a request for a structure editor function, whether it is entering data in a field or a more powerful logical function, such as ^DETAIL.

To illustrate how structure editor processing in context works, let us take the ^DETAIL function. ^DETAIL is a logical structure editor function that normally makes the structure editor activate the detail trigger.

  • When the user issues this function while the cursor is in a field, ^DETAIL is inserted into the event input queue (as with any other function issued by the user). The structure editor processes each entry in the event input queue as soon as that entry reaches the front of the queue.

  • When ^DETAIL reaches the front of the queue, the structure editor evaluates the current situation and, if it is valid in the context, processes the ^DETAIL function by activating the field-level or entity-level detail trigger.

  • If it is not valid in context, the structure editor may need to do other things first.

    For example, if the user positioned the cursor with the mouse on an occurrence that did not previously have focus and clicked ^DETAIL, the structure editor would first have to give focus to that occurrence, which means first activating the getFocus trigger.

    Also, if moving the cursor to the new field means leaving another field which contains data, the validate and loseFocus triggers may need to be activated for the previous field. Furthermore, if the last field edited in the previous occurrence was the last field of a primary or candidate key, the validateKey and leaveModifiedKey trigger are activated; and so on.

  • If the structure editor determines that ^DETAIL is valid in the context, it activates the field-level detail trigger. This is where your ProcScript influences what happens next.

    Any ProcScript in the detail trigger is passed to the ProcScript interpreter. If the field-level detail trigger does not contain any ProcScript, the structure editor activates the entity-level detail trigger instead. If there is no ProcScript in the entity-level detail trigger, the structure editor does nothing and control is returned to the user.

  • If an error situation occurs, it can negate further processing at any point in the structure editor's attempt to process a function.

    For example, the ProcScript in the loseFocus trigger might prevent the user from leaving the field with a simple return -1 statement, in which case ^DETAIL in another field is pointless. If this happens, the structure editor ignores the ^DETAIL function in the other field, and also refrains from activating any other triggers in the chain following the loseFocus.
